# Cache busting

## TLDR

For static assets, always use cache busting via filenames. Avoid stale content in browser cache. Good: `main.a1b2c3.js`. Bad: `main.js`.

## Problem

Browsers cache static assets by filename. New deployments with same filenames lead to users seeing stale content. Manual versioning is error-prone. Inconsistent state between HTML and assets causes application crashes.

## Good solution

Build tools appending content hashes to filenames. Serve hashed assets with long `Cache-Control` headers.

```html
<!-- Generated by build tool -->
<script src="/assets/index-D7b3A1.js"></script>
```

## Bad solution

Serving assets with static names without hashing.

```html
<script src="/assets/index.js"></script>
```

## Impact

- **[Reliability](../../home/impact/positive/reliability.md)**: Users always receive latest application version.
- **[Performance](../../home/impact/positive/performance.md)**: Immutable hashed files allow aggressive long-term caching.
- **[Consistency](../../home/impact/positive/consistency.md)**: Frontend assets synchronization guaranteed.

## Exceptions

- **API endpoints**: Cache-control headers manage dynamic content.
- **External CDN assets**: Third-party versioning strategy applies.

## References

- [MDN: Cache-Control by Mozilla Developers](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Cache-Control)
